examples.q: Two forces act on an object in the SAME direction: 30 N east and 20 N east. What is the resultant vector?
examples.solution:
When vectors point in the SAME direction,
you simply ADD their magnitudes.

The resultant keeps the same direction.

Step 1 — Add the magnitudes:
    30 N + 20 N = 50 N

Step 2 — Keep the direction:
    Direction = East

Answer:  Resultant = 50 N, East

This is called finding the NET vector.
Same direction → magnitudes add up.

examples.q: Two forces act on an object in OPPOSITE directions: 40 N east and 15 N west. What is the resultant vector?
examples.solution:
When vectors point in OPPOSITE directions,
you SUBTRACT the smaller from the larger.
The resultant points in the direction of the larger vector.

Step 1 — Assign signs (East = positive, West = negative):
    +40 N  and  −15 N

Step 2 — Add algebraically:
    +40 + (−15) = +25 N

Step 3 — Interpret the sign:
    Positive → East

Answer:  Resultant = 25 N, East

examples.q: Two vectors act at RIGHT ANGLES: 3 N north and 4 N east. Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant.
examples.solution:
When vectors are PERPENDICULAR, use the
Pythagorean Theorem to find the magnitude,
and tan⁻¹ for the direction.

Step 1 — Find the magnitude:
    R² = 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25
    R = √25 = 5 N

Step 2 — Find the direction angle from East:
    tan θ = opposite / adjacent
    tan θ = 3 (North) / 4 (East) = 0.75
    θ = tan⁻¹(0.75) ≈ 37°

Answer:  5 N at 37° North of East

questions.q: Two vectors point in the same direction: 14 N north and 11 N north. What is the resultant?
questions.given: Same direction → add magnitudes, keep direction.

questions.q: Two forces act in opposite directions: 60 N right and 25 N left. What is the resultant?
questions.given:
Opposite directions → subtract smaller from larger.
Resultant points in the direction of the larger vector.
questions.sol: 60 − 25 = 35 N in the direction of the larger force (Right).

questions.q: A vector of 50 N East and a vector of 50 N West are added. What is the resultant?
questions.given:
When two equal vectors oppose each other, they cancel out.
Assign East = positive, West = negative.
questions.sol: +50 + (−50) = 0 N. The vectors cancel — the object is in equilibrium.

questions.q: Two perpendicular vectors have magnitudes 6 N and 8 N. What is the magnitude of the resultant?
questions.given:
For perpendicular vectors:
    R² = A² + B²
    R = √(A² + B²)

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes a vector quantity?
questions.given:
A vector has both MAGNITUDE and DIRECTION.
A scalar has magnitude only.
questions.sol: Vectors require both magnitude and direction. '40 N eastward' specifies both.

questions.q: A person walks 10 m East then 10 m West. What is their total displacement?
questions.given:
Displacement is a vector — it has direction.
East = positive, West = negative.
Add algebraically.
questions.sol: +10 + (−10) = 0 m. The person returns to the starting point. Displacement = 0.

questions.q: Two perpendicular vectors measure 5 N and 12 N. What is the resultant magnitude?
questions.given:
Pythagorean Theorem for perpendicular vectors:
    R = √(A² + B²)

questions.q: A resultant vector of 10 N is formed by two perpendicular components. One component is 6 N. What is the other?
questions.given:
Rearrange the Pythagorean Theorem:
    B = √(R² − A²)

questions.q: Three forces act on an object in a straight line: 20 N right, 30 N right, and 10 N left. What is the net force?
questions.given:
Assign right = positive, left = negative.
Add all vectors algebraically.

questions.q: A vector pointing at 37° above the horizontal has a magnitude of 10 N. What is its vertical component?
questions.given:
Vertical component = R × sin θ
sin 37° ≈ 0.6
